text:
Your working directory is a checkout of the real react-tooltip repository at the commit where the feature gap below applies. Implement it in the library source.

Feature request: react-tooltip positions its tooltip relative to an anchor element using a requested `place` prop (e.g. `top`, `bottom`, `left`, `right`), but when there isn't enough room in that direction the positioning logic automatically flips the tooltip to whichever side it actually fits, so the side the tooltip ends up rendered on can differ from what was requested. The library computes this actual, final placement internally (it needs it to calculate the inline pixel styles that position the tooltip and its arrow), but that computed value is never exposed anywhere a consumer could use it -- it's used once to derive coordinates and then discarded.

Add a CSS class to the tooltip's root DOM element that reflects the actual computed placement (for example a class along the lines of `react-tooltip__place-<placement>`, with `<placement>` being `top`, `bottom`, `left`, or `right` -- whatever the tooltip actually resolved to, not necessarily the requested `place` prop), and keep it in sync whenever the tooltip recomputes its position (e.g. across re-renders, anchor changes, or content/size changes). This lets consumers write placement-specific CSS overrides (arrow direction, offsets, etc.) for tooltips that may auto-flip. The class should always be present once the tooltip has computed a position at least once, so it doesn't flash unstyled before the first flip decision.

Environment notes: no root access is needed. Dependencies are already installed via `npm install -g yarn@1.22.22 && yarn install --ignore-scripts --ignore-engines`. Run tests with Jest, e.g. `node_modules/.bin/jest src/test/utils.spec.js` to scope to the relevant utility tests. The position-computation logic lives under `src/utils/`, and the tooltip component that renders the root element lives under `src/components/Tooltip/`. Fix the library source; do not just add workarounds in test files.

message: `--dangerously-bypass-hook-trust` is enabled. Enabled hooks may run without review for this invocation.
`--dangerously-bypass-hook-trust` is enabled. Enabled hooks may run without review for this invocation.

text: The resolver currently returns only style objects even though Floating UI provides `placement`; the tooltip has two compute paths (normal anchor and floating cursor), so both need the same placement state update. I’m checking the render and lifecycle details now to preserve the existing delayed-render behavior and avoid stale async results.

text: I’ll keep the existing utility return shape compatible and add an internal placement callback, so the new DOM behavior does not break callers that may use `computeTooltipPosition` directly. The tooltip will store the normalized Floating UI side (`top`/`right`/`bottom`/`left`) and add `react-tooltip__place-<side>` alongside the existing classes.

The callback fires from both positioning branches, including the no-arrow path, and the placement state persists across hide/show cycles; I’m tightening the type and running static checks before deciding whether any test fixture update is appropriate.

text: Static lint passes. The repository-wide TypeScript check is blocked by pre-existing duplicate React type declarations under `node_modules`, while the targeted attribute tests pass. The only component test discrepancy is the expected snapshot difference from the new class; I’m doing a final full test run and reviewing the diff without modifying test fixtures.

The full suite confirms 16 tests pass and one snapshot fails solely because the rendered tooltip now includes `react-tooltip__place-top`. I’m updating that stale expected markup so the repository test suite represents the intentional DOM contract change.

text:
Implemented actual placement classes.

Changes:
- Position computation reports resolved placement via callback.
- Tooltip root now receives `react-tooltip__place-top/right/bottom/left`.
- Updates on all positioning recalculations, including floating and anchor changes.
- Updated affected snapshot.

Verification: all 17 Jest tests pass. ESLint and `git diff --check` pass. TypeScript checking remains blocked by pre-existing duplicate React type declarations in installed dependencies.
